opp.name: MPM Rent - eProc&Intake
opp.account: PT. Mitra Pinasthika Mustika Rent (MPM Rent)
opp.next_step: Initial meeting and Demo on 26th June with Pak Andianto and his team. Went well, impressed with the intake and how Bhasa Indonesia can be used for intake, Next steps to share high level pricing for internal approvals
opp.ais_why:
Dimension	Score (/10)	Assessment
AI Leadership Commitment	6.5	AI initiatives exist, but they're largely IT- and operations-focused rather than enterprise-wide.
Digital Transformation Maturity	7.5	Strong digital transformation journey across MPM Group with modernization of core systems.
Procurement Complexity	8.0	Large fleet, maintenance, OEM, leasing, and services procurement create meaningful sourcing complexity.
Enterprise Scale	7.5	Large Indonesian enterprise, but below the scale of Fortune 500-type global organizations.
Likelihood to Invest in AI Procurement	6.8	Moderate. AI investment appears to prioritize operational efficiency before procurement transformation.
Overall AIS	7.3/10	Moderately attractive prospect

buyer.account_name: PT. Mitra Pinasthika Mustika Rent (MPM Rent)

items.quote: high level pricing for internal approvals

items.area: Quantified business case (ROI, savings, cycle-time improvement)

items.quote: 0 buyer-side contact roles on this opp

metrics.narrative: No quantified business case or pain articulated in any recorded source. The 26 June demo (not recorded) focused on intake and Bahasa Indonesia capability, suggesting operational procurement efficiency is the use case, but no cost savings, cycle-time reduction, headcount impact, or ROI was documented in Next_Step__c or any task. The buyer's interest in intake and local-language capability implies a qualitative pain around manual procurement request handling, but this remains unquantified. Quantifying the value case (savings, FTE reduction, cycle-time improvement) is a required next step to justify the $150k investment and unlock internal approvals.

sources: Avoma manifest, 18 June and 26 June 2026 (attended both calls)
Next_Step__c, 26 June 2026 (requested pricing for internal approvals)
champion.narrative: Pak Andianto (Head of Procurement) is a developing champion. He attended both the 18 June intro call and 26 June demo, drove the demo agenda (focus on intake and Bahasa Indonesia), and explicitly requested high-level pricing for internal approvals, signaling active engagement and intent to advance the deal internally. However, his access to the Economic Buyer and Decision Maker is unconfirmed, and he has not yet demonstrated advocacy behaviors (introducing us to senior stakeholders, sharing internal docs, pushing back on competitors, or driving urgency). He is engaged but mid-level, and without exec sponsorship or multi-threading, the deal is at risk if he is overruled, deprioritized, or leaves. Developing Pak Andianto into a stronger champion (securing his explicit advocacy, mapping his internal influence, and leveraging him to reach the EB) is a required next step.

competition.narrative: No competitor named in any source. Competitors__c is blank, no Avoma call content (calls not recorded), no Next_Step__c or task mentions a rival, and no Closed Lost Reason or competitive field is populated. The competitive status is unknown: this could be a sole-source evaluation (buyer went direct to Zycus after a partner intro or referral), a quiet multi-vendor RFI where competitors have not yet surfaced in our conversations, or an incumbent replacement where the incumbent is unnamed. Given the APAC market and procurement transformation scope, likely competitors include SAP Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, or a local/regional vendor. Confirming the competitive landscape (sole-source vs multi-vendor, any incumbent being replaced, any other vendors the buyer is evaluating) is a required next step to position our wedge and pricing strategy.

sources: Next_Step__c, 26 June 2026 (demo feedback on intake + Bahasa)
identify_pain.narrative: Qualitative pain confirmed around intake and local-language adoption, but not quantified or tied to a pain owner. The buyer was 'impressed with the intake and how Bahasa Indonesia can be used for intake' (26 June demo), indicating operational pain around manual procurement request handling and user adoption barriers due to language. This suggests current-state inefficiency (likely manual intake, English-only tools, low user adoption), but no specific pain owner, cost of the pain (FTE hours, error rate, delay), or compelling event (contract expiration, audit finding, growth mandate) has been articulated. The pain is inferred from the demo focus, not explicitly stated by the buyer. Confirming the pain owner (Pak Andianto vs a senior sponsor), quantifying the pain, and identifying any forcing function are required next steps.

paper_process.narrative: No paper process, contracting mechanics, or legal/InfoSec requirements have been discussed. No MSA, SOW, procurement channel (direct vs partner-led), InfoSec review, or ERP integration scoping has been mentioned in any source. The deal is still pre-commercial (awaiting pricing), so paper process is not yet a blocker, but it is entirely unmapped. Given the APAC geography and partner involvement (Hawa Fanfa from the relevant stakeholder attended both calls), the contracting path (direct, via partner, or local entity) and any parent-company approval requirements should be clarified before commercial negotiation begins.

sources: Next_Step__c, 26 June 2026 (Andianto requested pricing 'for internal approvals')
Account-level contact roster (Div Head present but not confirmed on this opp)
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified. The mapped contact is Pak Andianto (Head of Procurement, andrianto.gunawan@mpm-rent.com), who attended both the 18 June intro call and 26 June demo and is driving the intake/eProcurement evaluation. However, he has requested high-level pricing 'for internal approvals', signaling he does NOT control the commercial decision himself. The approval authority (CFO, CEO, or Div Head) is unmapped. Account-level contacts include a Div Head of Procurement, Risk Management, Policy & Procedure and Quality Assurance, who may be the EB or a path to the EB, but no call or email confirms their involvement in this opportunity. Without EB access, pricing and ROI conversations cannot advance, and the deal risks stalling at mid-level or being deprioritized.

sources: Avoma manifest (two calls, both procurement-led, no escalation)
decision_process.narrative: No decision process documented. The buyer has requested 'high-level pricing for internal approvals' (26 June), confirming a multi-step internal approval process exists, but the steps, approvers, timeline, and gates are unknown. It is unclear whether this is a board-level approval, a CFO sign-off, a procurement committee review, or a parent-company (Mitra Pinasthika Mustika) governance process. The call sequence (18 June intro, 26 June demo, both with Procurement team only) has not escalated to senior stakeholders or commercial/legal functions, so the process is still early and procurement-led. No timeline, approval milestones, or decision date have been stated. Mapping the approval chain and timeline is a required next step to avoid indefinite delay after pricing is shared.

sources: Next_Step__c, 26 June 2026 (demo feedback on intake + Bahasa)
decision_criteria.narrative: Partial criteria inferred from demo feedback and product scope. The buyer was 'impressed with the intake and how Bahasa Indonesia can be used for intake' (26 June demo), indicating two confirmed decision criteria: (1) intake/request management capability, and (2) local-language (Bahasa Indonesia) support for user adoption. Products in scope (iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk) suggest a full-suite procurement transformation, but no structured evaluation framework, RFI/RFP criteria, or weighted scorecard has been documented. It is unclear whether AI/Merlin capability, risk management, or supplier collaboration are active criteria, or whether the buyer is running a formal vendor comparison. Confirming the full decision criteria and any formal evaluation process is a required next step.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.

Surfacing the Merlin/agentic narrative in the pricing conversation and gauging buyer appetite is a required next step.

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 14 is driven by Verdict 'Slowing' — momentum stalling. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 19 is carried by stage trend +0.74; preference strength +0.40 (weight 20). Offsetting it: momentum 43 vs stage-expected 50.
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 43 (slipping backward): buyer has gone quiet — no buyer-side activity in the last 30 days. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 4 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, exec and champion, commercial, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment 40 reflects real customer investment: Customer owns 2 open commitment(s); Customer shared internal process / commitments.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 10 is anchored on the stage win (19), nudged by commitment 40 and momentum 43, and dragged by risk 14. Read is Partial Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 11 June 2026, moved quickly to Qualified stage with intro call 18 June and demo 26 June (~5 days ago). Demo feedback was positive (impressed with intake and Bahasa Indonesia capability), and buyer explicitly requested high-level pricing for internal approvals. However, pricing commitment remains undelivered as of 1 July 2026 (~5 days overdue), and no further buyer engagement or internal action has been logged. Next_Step__c dated 26 June reflects rep-initiated outreach (the demo), not a two-way buyer touch post-demo. Engagement is cooling: pricing delivery is stalled, and no buyer follow-up or escalation has occurred.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling. Demo 26 June (~5 days ago) was positive, but pricing commitment remains undelivered, no buyer follow-up has occurred, and no further action is logged. Rep-initiated outreach (demo on 26 June) is not buyer engagement; buyer is waiting for pricing, and delay risks further cooling or buyer moving to competitor.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 11 June 2026, demo 26 June 2026 (~5 days ago), 20 days in Qualified stage as of 01 July 2026. Close date 31 December 2026 (183 days out) is realistic for a 12-15 month APAC enterprise cycle. However, pricing was promised post-demo and has not been delivered; buyer explicitly waiting for high-level pricing for internal approvals. One genuinely stalled action (pricing delivery overdue ~5 days) puts this in Slowing per verdict guide rails, not Close Date Risk.
evidence: Demo 26 June 2026 went well; buyer impressed with intake and Bahasa Indonesia capability (source: Next_Step__c, dated 26 June 2026)
Pricing commitment made 26 June 2026, not yet delivered as of 1 July 2026 (~5 days overdue)
No Economic Buyer or Decision Maker mapped; approval authority unknown
Single-threaded with Pak Andianto (Head of Procurement per account-level contact); no multi-threading beyond demo attendees
No decision timeline, approval workflow, or compelling event documented
No competitor named in any source; status (sole-source vs unknown competitive landscape) unclear
No contact roles on opportunity record; data hygiene gap
north_star_verdict.headline: Pricing commitment from 26 June demo (~5 days ago) remains undelivered; buyer awaiting high-level envelope for internal approval, engagement cooling. Qualified stage at 183 days to close is credible for APAC enterprise timeline, but stalled pricing delivery risks losing momentum to inaction or a competitor entering.

flags: Single-threaded with Pak Andianto; no contact roles on the opportunity record. Multi-thread to the Div Head of Procurement and other account-level contacts (Ameliza Bella, Sarani Dewi) to build redundancy and map the approval chain. Add all engaged buyer contacts to the opportunity contact roles (data hygiene + multi-thread signal).
Single-threaded with the relevant stakeholder; no multi-threading and no Economic Buyer or Decision Maker identified. High risk if primary contact leaves, deprioritizes, or is overruled.
Pricing commitment from 2026-06-26 demo overdue by 5 days; buyer awaiting delivery. Delay risks cooling further or buyer moving to alternative.
No competitor intel (blind to competitive dynamics)
No quantified business case (Metrics gap): buyer impressed with intake but no cost, cycle-time, or error-rate pain captured. Risk of internal approval stalling without ROI anchor.
No decision timeline or approval workflow documented; risk of indefinite delay after pricing is shared.
best_practice_check.summary: Three high-leverage win-strategy moves: (1) deliver overdue pricing immediately to restart momentum before the buyer cools further or a competitor enters; (2) use the pricing conversation to map the Economic Buyer and approval chain, converting mid-level engagement into exec sponsorship; (3) multi-thread beyond Pak Andianto to the Div Head and senior stakeholders to derisk single-point-of-contact dependency.

customer_preference.evidence: Buyer impressed with intake and Bahasa Indonesia capability (26 June demo); explicitly requested pricing for internal approvals, signaling intent to advance. Positive but not decisive; no comparison to competitors or explicit preference stated.

competitive_position.summary: No competitor documented from any source. Unknown whether sole-source or competitive evaluation. Demo enthusiasm for Bahasa Indonesia intake is a positive signal, but without competitive context the wedge is unanchored.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI/Merlin is in the product scope (Merlin included in Products__c), but no AI-specific positioning, use case, or buyer interest has been documented. The 26 June demo focused on intake and Bahasa Indonesia capability, with no mention of Merlin negotiation agent, I2O, or agentic AI. Given the procurement transformation scope and $150k deal size, AI should be a differentiator, but it is currently under-positioned. Bringing the Merlin/agentic story (e.g. AI-powered intake classification, negotiation agent on supplier price history, I2O for tail-spend automation) into the pricing conversation and ROI case is a required next step.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong product-market fit confirmed on intake and local-language capability; full-suite scope (iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, Merlin, iRisk) aligns with procurement transformation mandate, but fit on AI/Merlin, risk management, and supplier collaboration is unconfirmed.
